narrative:
I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimated a 47% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval. This model looks at patterns from thousands of past students to forecast bar exam success based on academic records, test scores, background, and other details like yours. In your case, you ranked in the 3rd decile for Year 1 grades—below the typical student's 6th decile—and a very low 1st decile for Year 3, compared to the average 6th decile there, signaling inconsistent academic performance relative to peers. Your LSAT score of 31 also fell well short of the dataset average of about 37, while your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 was right around the norm of 3.21. As a white male from an upper-middle-income family planning full-time studies, your demographics aligned closely with the majority in our data—whites make up over 80%, males about 56%, full-time over 90%—so they didn't stand out much.

The model's decision hinged most on a few key factors from your profile, ranked by their influence: your LSAT score had the strongest pull, nudging the prediction toward a higher risk of bar failure because it was lower than what succeeds for most students. Right behind it was your Year 3 grade decile, which similarly pushed the odds toward failure due to its unusually low rank. Your Year 1 decile came next, reinforcing that early academic struggles contributed to the concern. Family income and undergraduate GPA followed as moderate influences, both tilting slightly toward failure in your case, while demographics like gender, race, and full-time status had minimal sway.

Overall, your profile painted a picture riskier than the typical applicant who gets approved, mainly because your test score and later-year grades deviated downward from averages that correlate with bar passers. That 47% failure probability means you're on the edge—not a sure thing either way—but the model flagged these academic gaps as the biggest hurdles. Looking ahead, you could realistically boost your chances by targeting those weak spots: consider retaking the LSAT to aim for 35 or higher, as that's the top influencer you might improve with focused prep. Strengthening study habits to lift future grade deciles, perhaps through tutoring or better time management, would address the next biggest factors. Enrolling in rigorous bar prep courses early could also help overcome patterns seen in students with similar academic starts. With effort on these, you'd stand a much better shot at passing the bar and reapplying successfully—many students turn things around just like this. Keep pushing; your average GPA shows you have a solid foundation to build on.
